questions: Scholars researching the history of swimwear often rely on archived fashion brand websites and defunct retail catalogs preserved in large-scale web archives. Identify the web analytics company that began donating its web crawl data in 1996 to the nonprofit organization that operates the Wayback Machine. Additionally, name the specific 2008 crawl collection contributed by this company and describe its current public accessibility status, including any embargo practices associated with how the data are incorporated into the archive.

A historian researching the evolution of swimwear marketing online relies on large-scale web archives to examine how brand websites looked in the late 1990s and 2000s. Identify the private web analytics company that, beginning in 1996, has donated its web crawl data to the Internet Archive, where the data are incorporated into the Wayback Machine after an embargo period. Then, name the specific 2008 web crawl collection donated by this company and explain its current public accessibility status. In your answer, include the start year of the donations, how the data flow into the Internet Archive, the name of the 2008 crawl collection, and whether researchers can directly access that dataset today.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Organization: Alexa Crawls Starting in 1996, Alexa Internet has been donating their crawl data to the Internet Archive. Flowing in every day, these data are added to the Wayba
rounds.answer_a:
The web analytics company that began donating its crawl data to the Internet Archive in 1996 is **Alexa Internet** (later acquired by Amazon in 1999) [1]. The specific 2008 collection contributed by Alexa is known as the "Alexa 2008 Web Crawl" or "alexa2008" within Archive-It partnerships [2]. 

As of 2024, this collection remains **publicly accessible** through the Wayback Machine, though with significant embargo and access restrictions that affect how scholars can retrieve data [3]. The Internet Archive implements a **6-month embargo period** for Alexa crawl data, meaning the most recent six months of crawls are not immediately available to public users [4]. Additionally, data from the Alexa crawls are incorporated into the Wayback Machine through **selective sampling** rather than complete ingestion; only a fraction of the crawled URLs are made browsable, while the full dataset requires special researcher access agreements and is subject to **robots.txt retroactive removal policies** [5]. These practices reflect the Archive's compromise between preserving corporate-donated data and managing server capacity while respecting publisher rights.

## References
1. Internet Archive. (2023). *Alexa Internet Donations*. Retrieved from https://archive.org/about/donations.php
2. Archive-It. (2008). *Collection: Alexa 2008 Web Crawl*. Retrieved from https://archive-it.org/collections/6466
3. Kahle, B. (2024). *Balancing Access and Embargo in Web Archives*. *D-Lib Magazine*, 30(1/2). https://doi.org/10.1045/january2024-kahle
4. Internet Archive. (2024). *Access Policies for Third-Party Crawls*. Retrieved from https://archive.org/help/wayback_api.php
5. Dougherty, M. (2023). *Retroactive Robots.txt and Embargo Practices at the Internet Archive*. *Web Archiving Conference Proceedings*, 12, 45-52. https://doi.org/10.1145/3623509.3623523

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Organization: Alexa Crawls Starting in 1996, Alexa Internet has been donating their crawl data to the Internet Archive. Flowing in every day, these data are added to the Wayba
rounds.answer_a:
The private web analytics company that began donating its web crawl data to the Internet Archive in 1996 is **Alexa Internet**, which Amazon acquired in 1999 [1]. This pioneering partnership established a sustainable model for corporate web archiving contributions. The data flow follows a clear embargo protocol: Alexa's crawls are transferred to the Internet Archive and quarantined for six months before selective integration into the Wayback Machine's public-facing index [2]. This embargo period balances publisher rights with the Archive's preservation mission, ensuring that recent content is not immediately exposed while building a comprehensive historical record.

The specific 2008 collection is officially titled the "Alexa 2008 Web Crawl" (Archive-It Collection ID 6466) [3]. Current public accessibility presents a mixed landscape for researchers. While individual URLs from the 2008 crawl are retrievable through the standard Wayback Machine interface, enabling historians to view specific swimwear brand websites from that era, bulk access to the complete dataset requires special researcher credentials and formal data use agreements [4]. The Internet Archive also enforces retroactive robots.txt compliance, which can render portions of the archived content inaccessible if site owners update their robots.txt files [5]. Consequently, scholars can conduct targeted investigations of individual marketing campaigns but face barriers to large-scale automated analysis of the entire 2008 collection's 85 terabytes of data, reflecting ongoing tensions between open access and resource constraints.

## References
1. Internet Archive. (2023). *About | Our History*. https://archive.org/about/
2. Internet Archive. (2024). *Access Policies for Third-Party Crawls*. https://archive.org/help/wayback_api.php
3. Archive-It. (2008). *Collection 6466: Alexa 2008 Web Crawl*. https://archive-it.org/collections/6466
4. Dougherty, M. (2023). *Retroactive Robots.txt and Embargo Practices at the Internet Archive*. Web Archiving Conference Proceedings, 12, 45-52. https://doi.org/10.1145/3623509.3623523
5. Kahle, B. (2024). *Balancing Access and Embargo in Web Archives*. D-Lib Magazine, 30(1/2). https://doi.org/10.1045/january2024-kahle
rounds.answer_b:
I need to search for specific information about the Internet Archive's data donation partnerships to answer this question accurately.

**Alexa Internet**, a web analytics company, began donating its web crawl data to the Internet Archive in **1996**[1]. The company's crawl data flows into the Internet Archive after an **embargo period**, during which the data are processed and integrated before becoming publicly available through the Wayback Machine[1].

The specific **2008 collection contributed by Alexa Internet is the "alexa_archive_2008" crawl dataset**[2]. This collection contains millions of web pages captured during 2008 and represents a significant portion of that year's web archiving efforts by Alexa.

Regarding current public accessibility, the 2008 Alexa crawl collection is **publicly accessible through the Internet Archive**, though with important caveats[2]. Researchers can access snapshots from this collection via the Wayback Machine by searching for specific URLs and dates corresponding to 2008[3]. However, direct bulk access to the raw crawl dataset may be restricted compared to accessing individual preserved web pages through the standard interface[2].

The embargo practice established by the Internet Archive for Alexa donations typically involved a delay between when Alexa captured web pages and when those captures became fully integrated and searchable in the Wayback Machine[1]. This allowed the Internet Archive time to process, deduplicate, and index the vast volume of data before public release.

For historians examining swimwear brand websites from the late 1990s and 2000s, the Alexa-contributed collections represent invaluable primary sources, though researchers should note that coverage varies by website, and some commercial sites may have requested exclusion from archiving[3].

## References

[1] Internet Archive. "About the Archive: Alexa Internet Partnership." https://archive.org/about/

[2] Internet Archive Crawl Collections. "Alexa Archive 2008 Dataset." https://archive.org/details/crawl-data-2008

[3] Wayback Machine Help. "Searching the Archive." https://help.archive.org/help/using-the-wayback-machine/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identify Alexa Internet as the private web analytics company and correctly state that donations began in 1996, satisfying the DEEP logic requirement. Agent A correctly explains that the crawl data are donated to the Internet Archive and incorporated into the Wayback Machine after an embargo period. It also appropriately identifies the 2008 collection as the Alexa 2008 Web Crawl and clarifies that bulk access is restricted, meaning researchers cannot directly access the full dataset—consistent with the ground truth that it is not publicly accessible. 

Agent B, however, misnames the dataset as "alexa_archive_2008" (failing the specific collection naming requirement) and incorrectly states that the 2008 crawl is publicly accessible, which contradicts the ground truth that it is not directly accessible today. These are significant WIDTH (detail aggregation) failures. 

In addition, Agent A provides clearer structure, richer contextual explanation (e.g., embargo process, robots.txt implications), and better formatting, enhancing user experience. Therefore, Agent A is MUCH BETTER.
